Ada Lovelace wrote the first published computer algorithm in the 1840s, for a mechanical machine that was never actually built.
ENIAC, completed in 1945, weighed about 30 tons, filled a large room, and its first programmers were six women.
Before John Glenn orbited Earth, he asked NASA mathematician Katherine Johnson to check the computer's trajectory numbers by hand.
Apollo astronauts flew to the Moon with a guidance computer that had about 4 kilobytes of working memory.
Apollo flight software was woven by hand. Workers threaded wires through magnetic cores, so engineers called it little old lady memory.
IBM's first hard drive, built in 1956, stored 5 megabytes and weighed about a ton. Customers leased it rather than buying it.
The first message sent on the ARPANET in 1969 was supposed to be the word login. The system crashed after two letters, so it read lo.
Email is older than the web by about twenty years. Ray Tomlinson picked the at sign for addresses back in 1971.
The first spam email went out in 1978 to a few hundred ARPANET users, advertising computers. Recipients complained immediately.
Symbolics dot com became the first registered internet domain in March 1985, and it is still online today.
The first website ever made is still up at its original CERN address, explaining what the World Wide Web is.
Tim Berners-Lee's web server carried a handwritten sticker warning: this machine is a server, do not power it down.
Tim Berners-Lee has admitted the double slash in web addresses serves no real purpose, and says he regrets it.
The first webcam watched a coffee pot at Cambridge University, so researchers could see whether a trip downstairs was worth it.
Nearly all intercontinental internet traffic travels through cables lying on the ocean floor, not through satellites.
Sharks have been filmed biting undersea internet cables, which is partly why some cables get extra protective wrapping.
Wi-Fi does not stand for anything. A branding firm invented the name because the technical standard was too clumsy to market.
Bluetooth is named after a tenth century Danish king, Harald Bluetooth, and its logo combines his two runic initials.
Hollywood star Hedy Lamarr co-invented a frequency hopping technique in 1942 that underpins modern wireless communication.
Douglas Engelbart built the first computer mouse out of wood. His patent expired before mice caught on, so he earned almost nothing.
In one 1968 demo, Douglas Engelbart showed off the mouse, video conferencing, and hypertext. It is now called the Mother of All Demos.
Xerox researchers invented the graphical interface, Ethernet, and the laser printer, then watched other companies get rich on them.
Apple's third co-founder, Ronald Wayne, sold his ten percent stake for 800 dollars in 1976. Today it would be worth hundreds of billions.
The Apple 1 computer went on sale for 666 dollars and 66 cents. Steve Wozniak said he simply liked repeating digits.
Before Apple existed, Steve Jobs and Steve Wozniak sold illegal blue boxes that tricked phone networks into giving free calls.
A toy whistle from Cap'n Crunch cereal boxes produced the exact 2600 hertz tone that unlocked free calls on the phone network.
Nokia began as a paper mill in Finland in 1865, more than a century before it made mobile phones.
Samsung started out in 1938 as a small trading company selling groceries, including dried fish and noodles.
The first mobile phone call was made in 1973 by Martin Cooper of Motorola, who used it to call his biggest rival at Bell Labs.
Motorola's first commercial mobile phone cost 3,995 dollars and offered about 30 minutes of talk time.
The first text message ever sent simply read Merry Christmas. It went out over the Vodafone network in December 1992.
IBM's Simon, released in 1994, was the first smartphone. It had a touchscreen, email, and apps, thirteen years before the iPhone.
Clocks on GPS satellites gain about 38 microseconds a day due to relativity. Without Einstein's corrections, GPS would drift kilometers daily.
Sputnik, the first satellite, was a metal sphere about the size of a beach ball, and amateur radio operators everywhere could hear it beep.
More than half of all active satellites orbiting Earth today belong to a single company, SpaceX.
Voyager 1's onboard computers hold about 69 kilobytes of memory, yet the probe still phones home from interstellar space.
Hubble's main mirror was ground with extreme precision to slightly the wrong shape. Astronauts later fitted corrective optics in orbit.
NASA's Mars rovers run on radiation hardened PowerPC chips similar to the processor in a late nineties iMac.
Ingenuity, the little helicopter that flew on Mars, ran Linux and used a smartphone class processor.
The Mars Climate Orbiter burned up in 1999 because one engineering team used metric units and another used imperial.
An Ariane 5 rocket destroyed itself in 1996 because of a software overflow in guidance code reused from its slower predecessor.
The United States coordinated parts of its nuclear arsenal using 8 inch floppy disks until 2019.
Adding one leap second in 2012 crashed Reddit, LinkedIn, and the reservation system of an entire airline.
Many older systems will hit trouble on January 19, 2038, when 32 bit clocks counting seconds since 1970 finally overflow.
Fixing the year 2000 bug cost an estimated 300 billion dollars worldwide, which is a big part of why so little actually broke.
The Morris worm of 1988 knocked out roughly ten percent of the early internet. Its author is now a professor at MIT.
The first PC virus, called Brain, appeared in 1986 and politely included the names, address, and phone number of its authors.
An early virus called Creeper taunted users with: I'm the creeper, catch me if you can. A program named Reaper was written to hunt it.
The ILOVEYOU virus infected millions of computers in 2000, but its author walked free because his country had no law against malware.
A researcher stopped the global WannaCry ransomware outbreak by registering an unclaimed domain name that cost about ten dollars.
The Stuxnet worm physically destroyed Iranian uranium centrifuges by quietly commanding them to spin themselves apart.
The Mirai botnet that broke much of the American internet in 2016 was built by college students feuding over Minecraft servers.
In 2000, a fifteen year old calling himself Mafiaboy knocked Yahoo, CNN, and eBay offline from his home in Montreal.
After watching WarGames in 1983, President Reagan asked whether it could really happen, which helped spark the first American cybersecurity directive.
Passwords were stolen almost as soon as they were invented. In the 1960s an MIT student printed everyone's passwords to get more computer time.
Year after year, the most common password found in data breaches is still 123456.
Britain's Colossus broke Nazi codes in 1944 but stayed a state secret for three decades, so other machines took the credit.
Claude Shannon, the father of information theory, was known for riding a unicycle while juggling through the halls of Bell Labs.
Grace Hopper handed out foot long pieces of wire to show people exactly how far light travels in one nanosecond.
Half a byte, four bits, is officially called a nibble.
The first microprocessor, Intel's 4004, was originally designed for a Japanese desk calculator.
A modern phone chip can hold well over 15 billion transistors. The first microprocessor in 1971 had 2,300.
JavaScript was created in about ten days in 1995, and it was named after Java purely for marketing. The two languages are unrelated.
Python the programming language is named after Monty Python's Flying Circus, not the snake.
Linus Torvalds announced Linux in 1991 as just a hobby that would never be big and professional. It now runs most of the internet.
All 500 of the world's fastest supercomputers run Linux.
Linus Torvalds wrote the first working version of Git in about ten days, and joked that he names all his projects after himself.
Much of the world's banking still runs on COBOL, a programming language designed in 1959.
Deep Blue beat world chess champion Garry Kasparov in 1997, the first match victory by a computer over a reigning champion.
During its 2016 Go match, AlphaGo played a move so strange that commentators assumed it was a mistake. Players still study move 37.
The Konami code exists because its creator found his own game too hard while testing it, so he built in a cheat and left it in.
The first video game Easter egg was hidden in Atari's Adventure by a programmer whose company refused to credit developers by name.
Level 256 of Pac-Man collapses into garbled chaos because the level counter overflows a single 8 bit byte.
Space Invaders speeds up as you destroy aliens by accident. The hardware drew fewer objects faster, and the designer kept the bug.
Tetris was created in the Soviet Union in 1984, and its inventor received no royalties for about a decade.
A Game Boy flew to the Mir space station in 1993, where a cosmonaut played Tetris in orbit. The console later sold at auction.
Atari buried hundreds of thousands of unsold game cartridges in a New Mexico landfill. Excavators dug them up in 2014.
The PlayStation began life as a Nintendo partnership. When Nintendo backed out publicly, Sony turned the prototype into a rival console.
The United States Air Force once built a supercomputer out of 1,760 PlayStation 3 consoles.
Windows Solitaire was designed partly to teach a generation of office workers how to drag and drop with a mouse.
Comic Sans was drawn for a friendly cartoon dog in Microsoft Bob, but the software shipped before the font was finished.
David Bradley, the engineer who invented Control Alt Delete, quipped that he created it, but Bill Gates made it famous.
The rolling green hill from the Windows XP wallpaper is a real, barely edited photograph taken in Sonoma County, California.
Google was originally called BackRub, and its current name is a misspelling of googol, a one followed by a hundred zeros.
Google's first storage rack was partly built from LEGO bricks, holding ten hard drives of 4 gigabytes each.
Amazon was nearly called Cadabra, until a lawyer misheard the name as cadaver over the phone.
In Amazon's early days as an online bookstore, a bell rang in the office every time a customer placed an order.
The first item sold on eBay was a broken laser pointer. The buyer explained that he collected broken laser pointers.
The first banner ad ran in 1994, and about 44 percent of the people who saw it clicked on it.
The first YouTube video, Me at the zoo, is nineteen seconds of a co-founder standing in front of elephants.
Twitter's original 140 character limit came from squeezing posts into a single SMS text message.
The hashtag was suggested to Twitter by a user in 2007. The company initially dismissed the idea as too nerdy.
Steve Wilhite, who created the GIF image format, insisted for decades that it is pronounced jif, like the peanut butter.
Emoji were created in Japan in 1999, and the original set of 176 tiny pictures now sits in the Museum of Modern Art in New York.
The at symbol has colorful names worldwide. Danes call it an elephant's trunk, Italians a snail, and Germans a spider monkey.
QR codes were invented in 1994 by an engineer at a Toyota supplier, who took inspiration from the board game Go.
The first product ever scanned with a barcode was a pack of Juicy Fruit gum, in an Ohio supermarket in 1974.
The first digital camera, built at Kodak in 1975, weighed nearly four kilograms and took 23 seconds to record one photo to cassette tape.
Project Gutenberg began in 1971 when a student typed the Declaration of Independence into a mainframe, creating the first e-book.
GitHub buried snapshots of thousands of open source projects on archival film in an Arctic mine, meant to last 1,000 years.
The word robot comes from a Czech word for forced labor, coined in a 1920 stage play about artificial workers.
Toy Story in 1995 was the first fully computer animated feature film. Some frames took more than 15 hours to render.
A single Wikipedia bot called Lsjbot has created millions of articles, mostly short entries about species and places.
In 2010, a programmer paid 10,000 bitcoin for two pizzas. At later market peaks, those coins were worth hundreds of millions of dollars.
Bitcoin's creator, known only as Satoshi Nakamoto, has never been identified, and an estimated fortune sits untouched in early wallets.
The internet's address book rests on just 13 root name server identities, though hundreds of machines worldwide answer for them.
